name: Pickled Turnips (Lift)
subtitle: Lebanon's electric-pink pickled turnips
blrNote: Turnips (shalgam) available at KR Market, Hopcoms, and larger sabziwalas - they're a winter staple but available most of the year in BLR. Beetroot is always available and cheap. At BLR temperatures, expect a good ferment in 5–7 days.

ingredients.name: Turnips
ingredients.unit: g, peeled and cut into sticks or wedges
substitutions: Radish (mooli) - different flavour but works with the technique

IN.where: KR Market, Hopcoms, Nature's Basket - more available in winter months (Nov–Feb)

equipment.name: Weight to keep vegetables submerged
equipment.notes: Small zip-lock bag with brine, or a plate

tldr: Turnip sticks + a slice of beetroot + salt brine = shocking pink pickles that every Lebanese table considers essential.

steps.title: Prepare the brine
steps.instruction: Dissolve 30g non-iodised salt in 1 litre of water. Stir until clear.
tips: This is approximately a 3% brine - the standard for most vegetable ferments
Use filtered or boiled-and-cooled water - chlorine in tap water can inhibit fermentation.
Warm water dissolves salt faster, but let it cool to room temperature before pouring over vegetables.

steps.title: Cut the vegetables
steps.instruction: Peel turnips and cut into sticks (about finger-width) or wedges. Peel the beetroot and slice into thin rounds or chunks.
tips: The beetroot is primarily for colour - you don't need much
Wear gloves when handling beetroot unless you want pink fingers for a day
Cut turnip sticks roughly the same thickness so they ferment at the same rate - thin pieces will go mushy while thick ones are still crunchy.
steps.checkpoint: Turnip sticks and beetroot slices ready

steps.title: Pack the jar
steps.instruction: Place beetroot slices at the bottom and interspersed through the jar. Pack turnip sticks vertically (standing up). Add garlic if using. Pour brine over until everything is submerged.
tips: Vertical packing keeps turnips submerged more easily
Tuck beetroot slices between the turnip layers rather than just at the bottom - the colour distributes more evenly throughout the jar.
steps.checkpoint: Jar packed with turnips and beet, submerged in brine

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover loosely. Leave at room temperature away from sun. The brine will turn pink within hours as beetroot bleeds. Taste from day 5.
tips: The colour transformation is dramatic - brine goes from clear to vivid pink within 24 hours
In BLR, 5–7 days usually produces the right balance of crunch and sourness
Bubbles indicate active fermentation - normal and good
steps.checkpoint: Shocking pink brine, turnips are sour-tangy and still slightly crunchy

steps.title: Refrigerate
steps.instruction: When turnips are tangy and still have some crunch, seal and refrigerate. The pink deepens over time.
tips: The brine is also delicious - use it in salad dressings or as a probiotic shot
Serve alongside falafel, shawarma, or grilled meats - the sour crunch cuts through rich and fatty foods perfectly.
steps.checkpoint: Vibrant pink pickled turnips, ready to serve

images.caption: Pickled Turnips (Lift)
images.attribution: Photo by Micah Tindell on Unsplash
images.alt: Pickled Turnips (Lift) - Lebanon's electric-pink pickled turnips

culturalContext.story: In Lebanon, Syria, and across the Levant, no shawarma stand, falafel shop, or family table is complete without a jar of bright pink pickled turnips. They're called "lift" (لفت) - Arabic for turnip - and their shocking magenta colour (from beetroot) makes them one of the most visually striking ferments in any cuisine.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Vegetable pickling in brine has been practiced in the Levant for thousands of years. The region's hot, dry climate made preservation essential. Turnips, being a hardy root vegetable that stores well and grows in poor soil, became a natural candidate for fermentation.
culturalContext.significance: Lift demonstrates how a single addition - beetroot for colour - can elevate a simple pickle into something iconic. The technique is identical to basic brine pickles, but the result looks and feels completely different. It's a masterclass in how presentation transforms food culture.
culturalContext.funFact: The pink colour of lift is so associated with Lebanese cuisine that food bloggers call it "Lebanese pink." The colour comes from betalain pigments in beetroot - the same compounds that make beet juice a natural dye used in cosmetics and food colouring.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Turnip availability in BLR
thingsToAccountFor.description: Turnips are most abundant November–February but available sporadically year-round. Ask your sabziwala or check Hopcoms. If unavailable, radish (mooli) works as an alternative.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Beetroot stains everything
thingsToAccountFor.description: Beetroot will stain hands, cutting boards, and countertops. Use a glass cutting board or line your workspace with newspaper. Wear old clothes.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Don't over-ferment
thingsToAccountFor.description: Turnips go from crunchy-sour to mushy-sour faster than cucumbers. In BLR heat, check at day 5 and refrigerate when they still have some bite.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Drain pickled turnips, slice thin, dehydrate at 40°C for 8–10 hours.
dehydratorIntegration.result: Pink turnip chips - sour, earthy, stunning colour. Conversation-starter snack.
tips: The colour fades slightly when dehydrated but remains visibly pink
Sprinkle with za'atar before dehydrating for extra flavour

variations.name: Spiced lift
variations.description: Add a dried red chilli, bay leaf, and a few peppercorns to the brine. Subtle warmth behind the tang.

variations.name: Mooli lift (Indian fusion)
variations.description: Use Indian radish (mooli) instead of turnip. Same beet-brine technique. The radish's peppery bite adds dimension.

variations.name: Quick-pink pickled onions
variations.description: Slice red onions, add a piece of beetroot, cover in the same brine. Ready in 3 days. Incredible on kebabs.
